prompt:
I run the puzzle page for our company newsletter and I generate the weekly maze by hand like an animal. I want a Go library that gives me the SAME maze for the same issue number, forever, on any machine — reproducibility is the whole point, so the random source and the carving walk are spelled out below and are not up for debate. Module go-mazekit, package mazekit at the root, stdlib only.

Randomness is a fixed LCG over unsigned 64-bit ints kept below 2^31: starting from state = seed, each draw does state = (state*1103515245 + 12345) mod 2^31 FIRST, then yields (state >> 16) mod n as the choice among n options. No math/rand anywhere.

Generate(w, h int, seed int64) (*Maze, error) — error if w or h < 1 or seed < 0. Cells are (x,y) with (0,0) top-left, x growing east, y growing south; entrance (0,0), exit (w-1,h-1). Carve with the classic recursive-backtracker, exactly like this: push (0,0) on a stack and mark it visited; while the stack isn't empty, look at the TOP cell, list its unvisited in-grid neighbours in the fixed order N, E, S, W; if there are none pop the cell, otherwise use one LCG draw over the list length to pick, knock the wall through, mark the neighbour visited, push it. That's it — one draw per carve, no draws when a dead end pops.

*Maze API: Width(), Height(); Open(x, y int, dir byte) bool saying whether the passage from (x,y) heading 'N'/'E'/'S'/'W' is open (border walls, out-of-range cells and junk directions are simply false — no panics); Render() string drawing the classic ASCII picture: 2h+1 lines of 2w+1 chars, '#' wall, ' ' carved, cell (x,y) at line 2y+1 column 2x+1, joined with \n, no trailing newline; Solve() string giving the moves 'N'/'E'/'S'/'W' from entrance to exit via BFS expanding neighbours in N, E, S, W order (1x1 maze = empty string); RenderWithPath() string, which is Render() with every cell on the solution path AND every opening the path passes through drawn as '.'.

mazekit_test.go is the contract: it pins the full ASCII render and solve string for several (size, seed) pairs — if your LCG or walk order deviates by one draw, those go up in flames — plus spanning-tree structure checks (w*h-1 passages, everything reachable, passages agreeing from both sides) and legality-walks of your solutions. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass; the test file stays as-is.
